data.title: DatChat rebrands to Myseum
data.description:
• DatChat (NASDAQ:DATS) is rebranding to Myseum.
  • The company’s common stock and Series A warrants, currently listed on the Nasdaq Capital Market, will begin trading under the new stock symbols “MYSE” and “MYSEW”, respectively, effective before the stock market open on Monday, August 11, 2025.
  • DATS +9.17% premarket to $2.5.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: American Lithium appoints Gregory Barbier as CFO
data.description:
• Gregory Barbier appointed chief financial officer of American Lithium (TSXV:LI:CA) effective August 7, 2025.
  • Barbier succeeds Paul Charlish in the role.
  • Barbier joined the company as controller in October 2024.
  • Source: Press release

data.title: Chime Financial shares dip after inaugural quarterly earnings after IPO
data.description:
Chime Financial (NASDAQ:CHYM) stock slipped 2.2% in Friday premarket trading after the fintech delivered its first quarterly earnings as a publicly traded company and issued guidance for continued growth.

"This was a breakout first quarter as a public company for Chime (NASDAQ:CHYM), driven by accelerating year-over-year growth, expanding margins, and continued product execution," said CEO and Co-Founder Chris Britt.

The company issued guidance for full-year revenue of $2.135B-$2.155B  (midpoint $2.145B vs. consensus of $2.105B), adjusted EBITDA of $84M-$94M (midpoint $89M vs. $71M consensus), and adjusted EBITDA margin of 2%-3%.

For Q3 2025, it expects revenue of $525M-$535M (midpoint $530M vs. $517M consensus), adjusted EBITDA of $12M-$17M (midpoint $15M vs. $13.3M consensus), and adjusted EBITDA margin of 4%.

It also expects incremental adjusted EBITDA margin to return to mid-40% or higher by the fourth quarter of 2025.

Q2 GAAP net income was -$923.4M vs. $78K in last year's Q2.

Q2 adjusted EBITDA jumped to $16M from $3.10M a year ago.

Q2 revenue of $528M, beating the consensus of $502.8M, rose 37% Y/Y, with payments revenue rising 19% Y/Y to $366M and platform-related revenue rising 113% to $162M.

Purchase volume climbed 18% Y/Y to $32.4B.

Active members grew 23% Y/Y to 8.7M and average revenue per active member increased 12% to $245.

data.title: Visible Gold appoints Veronique Laberge CFO
data.description:
• Visible Gold Mines (TSXV:VGD:CA) (OTCPK:VGMIF) announced on Friday the appointment of Veronique Laberge as CFO.
  • Laberge replaces interim CFO Martin Dallaire, who will remain CEO of the company.
  • Laberge has more than 19 years of experience.

data.title: Miller Industries outlines $750M–$800M revenue target for 2025 amid demand pressures and revised outlook
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Miller Industries (MLR) Q2 2025

Management View

  • William G. Miller, President and CEO, reported that, "In the second quarter, we continued to face challenges in the market, predominantly related to industry-wide demand headwinds. Retail sales activity was down 20% quarter-over-quarter, resulting in a 30% decrease in order intake from our distributors." He attributed the decline to lower consumer confidence and elevated costs of ownership, noting persistent elevated field inventory in the distribution channel.
  • Miller indicated management is "closely monitoring and adjusting production levels to meet current demand and accelerate the reduction of channel inventory, taking significant steps to improve our costs and securing our supply chain to mitigate the long-term risks of tariffs."
  • Deborah L. Whitmire, Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer, stated, "Net sales for the second quarter of 2025 were $214 million versus $371.5 million for the second quarter of 2024, a 42.4% year-over-year decrease driven largely by a drop in chassis volumes after volumes were significantly elevated in the prior year period."
  • Whitmire added, "Gross profit was $34.6 million or 16.2% of net sales for the second quarter of 2025 compared to $51.1 million or 13.8% of net sales for the prior year period. The margin improvement was driven mainly by product mix with a higher percentage of body deliveries compared to chassis volumes."
  • Miller provided further updates, mentioning, "We are implementing targeted cost reduction initiatives in the second half of the year to better align our operational structure with current demand levels."
  • Miller also noted a strategic commitment: "As always, our top priority will be returning capital to shareholders through our industry-leading dividend, which will be paid for the 59th consecutive quarter."

Outlook

  • Miller stated, "Due to the heightened uncertainty and near-term challenges we've discussed, we are revising our previously issued guidance for the 2025 fiscal year. We now expect revenues in the range of $750 million to $800 million. And at this time, we are suspending guidance on earnings per share."
  • He noted that the revised revenue guidance "anticipates no changes in the current regulations or unknown effects of rapidly evolving tariff situation."
  • Management explained that the organization-wide operational initiatives being evaluated "could have a material impact on our cost structure, potentially resulting in extraordinary expenses and potential losses in the second half of the year."

Financial Results

  • Whitmire reported, "Net income for the second quarter of 2025 was $8.5 million or $0.73 per diluted share compared to net income of $20.5 million or $1.78 per diluted share in the prior year period."
  • She stated, "At the end of the second quarter, we had a cash balance of $31.8 million, up $4.4 million sequentially and up $7.5 million as of the end of last year."
  • Whitmire continued, "We also reduced our debt balance by $20 million, down to $55 million during the second quarter and has since paid down another $5 million bringing the current balance to $50 million."
  • Accounts receivable as of June 30, 2025, was $270.4 million compared to $292.6 million as of the end of last quarter.
  • Inventories at quarter-end were $165.5 million, while accounts payable stood at $98 million.

Q&A

  • Michael Shlisky, D.A. Davidson: Asked about cost structure reduction actions and how to balance temporary headwinds with capacity preservation. Miller responded, "We are analyzing all potential aspects of the business, both SG&A expenses, temporary expenses, long-term expenses; current projects that are underway... making sure that whatever changes that we make or cuts that we make along the way don't affect our long-term success."
  • Shlisky: Inquired about sales-side actions to spur demand. Miller replied, "We've worked strategically with our chassis partners to create incentives and programs... We are working diligently in all manners to help market and move the product, particularly more in the Class 5 model chassis."
  • Shlisky: Asked if an interest rate cut is included in guidance. Miller answered, "It would be potential upside. So currently, what we're looking at is sort of no change in the environment is what we're basing our predictions off of our future for the next few quarters."
  • Shlisky: Questioned share buyback program status and board sentiment. Whitmire stated, "There was $25 million originally on that buyback plan. We currently bought $5 million, so there's $20 million left on that. The Board evaluates that every quarter."
  • Shlisky: Sought updates on military RFQs and RFPs. Miller said, "The contract with the Canadian military was finally signed over the past quarter... we're still working on multiple larger RFQs, but none of those have gotten to the contract or being -- have been awarded at this time."
  • Shlisky: Asked about mix expectations and gross margin outlook. Miller commented, "We anticipate that chassis will start -- the mix will start to normalize over the next 2 quarters and into next year... We believe margins will settle back in the mid-13s as we move forward."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts raised persistent concerns about cost reductions, demand triggers, and the sustainability of gross margin improvements, reflecting a slightly negative to neutral tone, especially in light of suspended EPS guidance and revised revenue targets.
  • Management projected a tone of caution and careful adjustment, but also emphasized confidence in long-term fundamentals and financial discipline, as seen in Miller's statement: "We are confident we will be well positioned as the market environment improves and we will enter 2026 from a position of strength."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, there is a shift toward greater caution from both analysts and management, particularly due to revenue guidance reduction and EPS guidance suspension.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The revised revenue guidance for 2025 ($750 million to $800 million) represents a reduction from the prior quarter's guidance of $950 million to $1 billion.
  • EPS guidance was suspended this quarter, whereas last quarter it was reaffirmed in the range of $2.90 to $3.20 per diluted share.
  • Gross margin in Q2 improved to 16.2% of net sales from 15% in Q1, mainly driven by product mix, but management now expects margins to "settle back in the mid-13s" moving forward.
  • There is a greater emphasis on cost controls and operational adjustments this quarter, with management citing "targeted cost reduction initiatives" and the potential for extraordinary expenses.
  • Analysts' questions this quarter were more focused on immediate mitigation strategies and the timing of demand recovery, reflecting heightened concern about near-term performance versus last quarter’s relatively more optimistic tone.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management highlighted "industry-wide demand headwinds" and elevated inventory in the distribution channel as ongoing challenges.
  • Risks cited include lower consumer confidence, higher interest and insurance costs, tariff-related price increases, and regulatory barriers (particularly in California).
  • The company is actively implementing cost reduction initiatives and adjusting production to reduce channel inventory. Tariff surcharges and price increases on new orders and accessories have also been put in place to mitigate these risks.
  • Management indicated that "operational initiatives... could have a material impact on our cost structure, potentially resulting in extraordinary expenses and potential losses in the second half of the year."

Final Takeaway

Miller Industries management acknowledged a challenging market environment in Q2 2025, with demand headwinds, reduced order intake, and elevated channel inventories leading to a downward revision of full-year revenue guidance to $750 million–$800 million and suspension of EPS guidance. The company is proactively implementing cost reduction initiatives and adjusting production to accelerate inventory normalization, while maintaining a strong focus on free cash flow generation, debt reduction, and shareholder returns. Management remains confident in the company's long-term fundamentals and market position, anticipating recovery opportunities in commercial and military sectors, and committing to update stakeholders as operational and market conditions evolve.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: NLIGHT outlines 40% A&D revenue growth target for 2025 while advancing defense laser programs
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: nLIGHT, Inc. (LASR) Q2 2025

Management View

  • CEO Scott H. Keeney opened the call by highlighting a strong quarter for nLIGHT, with revenue, gross margin, and adjusted EBITDA all ahead of expectations. He stated that "results were primarily driven by our record aerospace and defense products and development revenue," with A&D revenue making up 66% of total sales in the quarter. Keeney described the company's position as "well positioned to drive continued growth in A&D with leading high-power laser technology developed over the past 2 decades across the entire technology stack from chips to full laser systems, which is supported by our U.S. manufacturing sites."
  • Keeney reported solid progress on the HELSI-2 program, a $171 million Department of Defense effort for a 1-megawatt high-energy laser, with shipment of critical components being a key driver of record defense product revenue. He also noted the near completion of work on the Army's DE M-SHORAD program and active engagement with new opportunities under the President's Golden Dome executive order focused on non-kinetic missile defense capabilities. Internationally, Keeney mentioned that "in the second quarter, we began shipping to a new international customer, and we have a growing pipeline of new global opportunities."
  • On the commercial side, Keeney indicated that revenue was ahead of expectations, driven by increases in both microfabrication and industrial. However, he cautioned, "we do not expect a sustained improvement in overall demand, particularly with respect to the majority of our industrial applications, and we continue to rationalize our investments in our commercial markets."
  • CFO Joseph Corso stated, "Our second quarter results were characterized by strong execution across the board. Better-than-expected revenue, a strong mix of business and higher factory absorption, coupled with exceptional execution from our manufacturing and operations teams drove meaningful upside to our gross margin."

Outlook

  • Management expects sequential growth in A&D revenue throughout the rest of 2025, with Keeney expressing "increased confidence that we can grow our revenues in aerospace and defense by at least 40% in 2025."
  • Revenue guidance for Q3 2025 is set in the range of $62 million to $67 million, with a midpoint of $64.5 million. Product revenue is expected to be approximately $45 million and development revenue $19 million.
  • Product gross margin for Q3 is expected in the range of 32% to 36%, while development gross margin is anticipated at approximately 8%, resulting in a total gross margin range of 24% to 30%. Adjusted EBITDA is guided in the range of $2 million to $6 million for the third quarter.

Financial Results

  • Q2 2025 total revenue was $61.7 million, an increase of 22% year-over-year and up 19.5% from Q1 2025. Aerospace and Defense revenue reached a record $40.7 million, up 48.6% year-over-year and 24% sequentially. Defense products revenue grew 74.5% year-over-year.
  • Commercial markets revenue was $21 million, down 9% year-over-year but up 11% sequentially. The increase was attributed to pent-up demand in microfabrication following the manufacturing transition out of China.
  • Total gross margin was 29.9%, products gross margin 38.5%, and development gross margin 13.1%.
  • GAAP net loss was $3.6 million or ($0.07) per share, compared to a net loss of $8.1 million or ($0.16) per share last quarter. Non-GAAP net income was $2.9 million or $0.06 per diluted share. Adjusted EBITDA stood at $5.6 million.
  • The company ended the quarter with $114 million in cash, cash equivalents, restricted cash, and investments.

Q&A

  • Daniel James Eggerichs, Craig-Hallum: Asked what drove A&D outperformance and margin strength. CFO Corso responded that "we just executed very well in the quarter," citing amplifier sales into the HELSI-2 program as a key factor and attributing margin outperformance to "a handful of small things that this quarter all went very well."
  • Eggerichs: Inquired about laser sensing programs and revenue outlook. Corso explained that existing platforms saw higher volumes due to customer restocking and that new, classified programs are progressing, with expected LRIP phase work in the second half.
  • Eggerichs: Asked about product gross margin outlook given Q2’s high 38.5% result. Corso said, "as we forecast going forward, right, we're not going to forecast that upside. Could that happen again? Yes, but that's not something that we're banking on nor do I think that as you're building your model, you should bank on."
  • James Andrew Ricchiuti, Needham: Sought views on 2026 and drivers of the 2025 outlook increase. Corso stated it is "a little early for us to really start talking about 2026" but cited broad-based growth in defense portfolio and HELSI-2 as contributors to the 2025 outlook.
  • Ruben Roy, Stifel: Asked if the increase in the A&D growth outlook to 40% was driven by HELSI-2. Corso confirmed, "there was an improvement on that end of it," noting capacity increases and knock-on effects for development work.
  • Rodney McFall, Northcoast Research: Asked about the international customer opportunity and execution risk. CEO Keeney described the opportunity as "material" and "all still in the early stages." McFall also asked about commercial market rationalization; Keeney explained that engineers are being shifted from industrial lasers to new defense-based lasers.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed for details on drivers of outperformance, the sustainability of high margins, and specifics around new business, reflecting a slightly positive but probing tone. There was interest in the durability of A&D growth and execution on new programs.
  • Management maintained a confident tone in prepared remarks, with Keeney stating "we are well positioned" and Corso highlighting "exceptional execution." In Q&A, responses were measured and transparent, with Corso saying, "we're not going to forecast that upside" and providing context on program transitions and risk.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone was more confident, particularly regarding the outlook for A&D growth and operational execution. Analysts remained constructive but sought additional clarification on sustainability and program timing.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for A&D revenue growth increased from "at least 25%" in Q1 to "at least 40%" in Q2, with the HELSI-2 program cited as a key driver.
  • Gross margin rose from 26.7% in Q1 to 29.9% in Q2, with product gross margin moving from 33.5% to 38.5%.
  • Sequential revenue growth was higher this quarter for both A&D and commercial segments.
  • Management’s confidence grew, with greater emphasis on broad-based defense growth and successful execution on program transitions. Analysts’ questions remained focused on growth drivers and risk, consistent with prior quarters.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged risks related to transitioning amplifier products from R&D to manufacturing, with Corso noting "this transition, while not without risk, is critical as we continue optimizing our amplifier production line for higher volumes."
  • CEO Keeney highlighted uncertainty in commercial demand and ongoing rationalization of investments in those markets.
  • The company continues to monitor execution risk with new international customers and program transitions. There was no reference to tariff risk in the current quarter’s transcript.

Final Takeaway

nLIGHT delivered a strong second quarter with record A&D revenue driven by the HELSI-2 program, improved gross margins, and better-than-expected results across product and development lines. Management raised A&D revenue growth expectations for 2025 to at least 40%, supported by a growing pipeline of defense programs and successful operational transitions. While commercial market demand remains uncertain and program scaling carries risk, the company is focusing resources on its core defense opportunities and expects continued sequential growth through the remainder of the year.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Oxford Lane Capital estimates NAV per share at $4.13-$4.23 as of July 31
data.description:
• Oxford Lane Capital (NASDAQ:OXLC) announced on Friday an unaudited estimated net asset value per share in the range of $4.13 to $4.23 as of July 31.
  • The stock was trading +0.93% pre-market to $3.27.
